text: The surgeon introduces the needle into the abdomen by grasping the suture instead of the needle, demonstrating a unique approach to laparoscopic suturing.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic instrument to grasp the suture approximately three centimeters behind the swedge of the needle, preparing for needle introduction.

The surgeon demonstrates the correct technique for grasping the suture, ensuring it is held approximately three centimeters behind the swedge to allow proper rotation of the needle during laparoscopic suturing.
The suture is picked up from a neutral position, with the needle oriented in the direction intended for sewing.
A right-handed surgeon uses their left hand to grasp the suture three centimeters from the swedge, positioning the needle tip to the left of the needle swedge.
The suture is introduced through a ten millimeter port, with the needle positioned for sewing.
The surgeon grasps the needle, ensuring it is held approximately three centimeters behind the swedge for optimal control and orientation.
The right hand grasps the needle one-third of the distance from the swedge to the tip, positioning it for optimal control during suturing.
The surgeon uses the needle to penetrate the tissue, following the previous step of grasping the needle with the dominant hand.
The surgeon positions the needle to enter the tissue at a sixty to seventy-five degree angle, similar to techniques used in open surgery.
The surgeon rotates the needle through the tissue, following the curve of the needle to ensure smooth penetration and accurate placement.
The surgeon uses axial needle drivers, such as Ethicon German needle drivers, to facilitate the grasping and manipulation of the needle during tissue penetration.
The surgeon uses the non-dominant hand to stabilize the tissue while the needle rotates through it, ensuring precise penetration and control during the suturing process.
The surgeon uses the non-dominant hand to grasp the tip of the needle as it emerges through the tissue, stabilizing the needle for further manipulation.
The surgeon uses a grasper to securely lock the needle in place, preventing accidental dropping during the procedure.
The dominant or right hand instrument is positioned to fall on top of the suture tag after completing the clockwise rotation, ensuring proper alignment for wrapping.
The surgeon positions the left hand instrument at the one o'clock position while the right hand instrument leaves the suture tag at the seven o'clock position.
The left hand moves back to the right side of the knot, positioning itself close to the tissue.
The right hand instrument rotates counterclockwise to grasp the tag of the suture, positioning it for the next step in the knot tying process.
The surgeon positions the left hand at the eleven o'clock position and the right hand at the five o'clock position, leaving the suture tag at the five o'clock position.
The surgeon performs a single clockwise wrap with the suture using the right hand instrument, completing the third and final wrap of the knot.
The surgeon's left hand is positioned to the left of the knot, while the right hand rotates clockwise around the suture, grasping the short tag.
The surgeon positions the left hand at the one o'clock position and the right hand at the seven o'clock position, manipulating the suture around the knot.
The surgeon completes the laparoscopic surgeon's knot, with the suture securely tied and positioned.
